summary: McKenzie shares his refund rate data from selling Bingo Card Creator, a $24.95 educational software product. Over six months, roughly 5% of customers requested refunds, though he considers this inflated by early software bugs, with the mature version seeing under 2%. He argues strongly for unconditional money-back guarantees, explaining that they actually increase sales by letting uncertain customers externalize risk. The most common refund reason — 'it doesn't do what I want' — proves the guarantee is working as intended, converting hesitant browsers into buyers.
key_points: Refund rates for mature shareware products can be under 2%, with 5% being the upper bound during buggy early releases
Unconditional money-back guarantees increase net sales by letting risk-averse customers buy before they're fully convinced
The most common refund reason ('doesn't do what I want') demonstrates the guarantee is converting otherwise-lost prospects into buyers
Processing refunds costs the developer nothing — PayPal refunds transaction fees on initiated refunds, making guarantees pure upside
money_quotes: If any customer were to ask for their money back after that period, I would go to any length of effort up to and including mailing them a personal check to get them their money back.
In the last six months, roughly 5% of my customers have requested a money-back guarantee.
For the most mature version of my software, my refund rate was under 2%.
It means that at least some of your customers are buying your product when they are not yet convinced that it will meet their needs.
The guarantee thus provides a safety net, allowing the customer to externalize the risk of their extended trial period to you, the developer.

They think 'Well, if it doesn't work out, I can always return it'. You should be happy to bear this 'risk', as a return costs you little or nothing to process, meaning that this is pure upside from your point of view.
I have never paid a penny as a result of processing a return.
key_insight: Money-back guarantees are pure upside for software sellers because they convert hesitant prospects into buyers while costing virtually nothing to honor.
